claim: The steel ingot was homogenized at 1200°C for 4 h, hot forged to 40 mm thickness, air-cooled, then reheated to 650, 750, 850, or 950°C for 2 h, hot rolled with 60% reduction to 16 mm, and air-cooled to produce samples R650, R750, R850, R950.
evidence_text: specimens were placed in a box furnace, heated to 650°C, 750°C, 850°C, and 950°C respectively, and held for 2 h. Subsequent hot rolling deformation was conducted with a 60 % reduction ratio, reducing the plate thickness from 40 mm to 16 mm (Fig. 1a). After air cooling, four groups of samples were obtained and named R650, R750, R850, and R950 in sequence.

claim: Thermo-Calc equilibrium calculations indicate that carbide precipitation/dissolution occurs ≤750°C, while DRX and DIFT occur ≥850°C.
evidence_text: Thermo-Calc software was used to calculate the equilibrium phase diagram and determine the phase composition at each temperature. The experimental design covered the critical temperature ranges for carbide precipitation/ dissolution (≤750°C) as well as DRX and DIFT (≥850°C).

claim: In R650, the volume fraction of C/F composite lamellae is 51.5±2.5%, δ-ferrite 48.5±2.5%, martensite 0%; in R750, C/F decreases to 43.3±3.0%, δ-ferrite increases to 56.7±3.0%.

In R650, ultra-fine spherical carbides have an average diameter of 158.3±76.9 nm and a distribution density of 1.7±0.2 μm⁻²; in R750, carbides coarsen to 247.3±86.8 nm, density decreases to 0.6±0.1 μm⁻² and exhibit coarse carbides at grain boundaries and fine carbides inside grains, consistent with Ostwald ripening.
evidence_text: In R650 sample (Fig. 3d-f), spherical carbides show a high-density and ultra-fine (average diameter: 158.3 ± 76.9 nm, distribution density: 1.7 ± 0.2 μm⁻²) within the lamella. When the temperature rised to 750 °C (Fig. 3g-i), the carbides undergo significant coarsening (average diameter increases to 247.3 ± 86.8 nm, distribution density: 0.6 ± 0.1 μm⁻²) and exhibit a distribution characteristic of “coarse carbides at grain boundaries + fine carbides inside grains”, which is consistent with Ostwald ripening [19,20].

claim: In the high-temperature rolling zone (R850, R950), spherical carbides completely dissolve, and the microstructure consists of δ-ferrite lamellae and honeycomb martensite lamellae enclosing equiaxed deformation-induced ferrite (DIF) grains.
evidence_text: In the high-temperature rolling zone, carbides dissolve completely, and the structure transforms into a duplex structure consisting of δ-ferrite lamellae (light) and honeycomb martensite lamellae (dark) (Fig. 2c-d), with numerous equiaxed ferrite grains (DIF) enclosed within honeycomb martensite lamellae.

claim: The volume fraction of honeycomb martensite increases from 33.2±3.6% in R850 to 59.0±4.2% in R950, and the equiaxed DIF grains coarsen.
evidence_text: as rolling temperature rises from 850 °C to 950 °C, the proportion of honeycomb martensite lamellae increases from 33.2 % to 59.0 %, while the equiaxed ferrite grains in martensite grow significantly.

claim: In low-temperature rolled samples, the average kernel average misorientation (KAM) is 0.48–0.55°, whereas in high-temperature rolled samples, KAM decreases to 0.3–0.4°, indicating lower dislocation density.
evidence_text: Samples in the low-temperature rolling zone exhibit an average KAM value of 0.48–0.55° (Fig. 4e-f), indicating a high dislocation density. In contrast, the KAM value of samples in the high-temperature rolling zone decreases to 0.3–0.4° (Fig. 4g-h).

claim: The proportion of grains with GOS < 2° increases from 6.9–12.3% in low-temperature rolled samples to 19.4–25.8% in high-temperature rolled samples, suggesting more recrystallized/new grains.
evidence_text: Comparison of the proportion of GOS (grain orientation spread) < 2° in the material shows that this proportion increases significantly in the high-temperature rolling zone (19.4–25.8 %) compared with the low-temperature rolling zone (6.9–12.3 %).

claim: The dominant crystallographic texture shifts from <001> in the low-temperature rolled samples to <101> in the high-temperature rolled samples, indicating the formation of recrystallized and DIFT grains.
evidence_text: the grain orientation changes (Fig. 4i-l), with the dominant orientation shifting from <001> (predominant in the low-temperature rolling zone) to <101>. This indicates that high-temperature rolling induced the formation of numerous new grains, including recrystallized grains [22] and products of deformation-induced ferrite transformation.

claim: In the low-temperature rolling zone, the nano-hardness of the carbide/ferrite composite lamellae is 3.64 GPa, significantly higher than that of δ-ferrite lamellae (2.92–2.93 GPa), yielding a hardness mismatch of 0.72 GPa.
evidence_text: In the low-temperature rolling zone, the hardness of the carbide/ferrite composite lamellae (3.64 GPa) is significantly higher than that of the δ-ferrite lamellae (2.92–2.93 GPa), with a hardness difference of 0.72 GPa.

claim: In the low-temperature rolling zone, carbide particles pin dislocations via the Orowan mechanism, increasing work hardening rate; in the high-temperature zone, the large hardness mismatch (max 3.86 GPa) induces stress concentration at phase boundaries, making them preferential crack initiation sites.
evidence_text: This hardness mismatch induces two key effects. In the low-temperature rolling zone, carbide particles pin dislocations via the Orowan mechanism, leading to an increase in the work hardening rate of the composite lamellae. In the high-temperature rolling zone, the large hardness difference between phases causes stress concentration at phase boundaries, which become preferential sites for crack initiation [23].

claim: The R650 specimen achieves a room-temperature Charpy impact absorbed energy of 207.5±29.3 J, an elongation of 19.0±3.2%, and a strength-plasticity product (SPP) of 16.51 GPa·%, with a crack initiation energy (Ei) of 59.6 J and a crack propagation energy (Ep) of 147.9 J indicating high resistance to both crack formation and unstable propagation.
evidence_text: the room-temperature IAE of the R650 specimen reaches 207.5 ± 29.3 J (Table 4), with an elongation of 19.0 ± 3.2 % and a SPP as high as 16.51 GPa⋅%. In the Charpy impact test, the impact load-displacement curve of R650 (Fig. 5b) shows an obvious toughness propagation stage, where the crack Ei (initiation energy) and Ep (propagation energy) reach 59.6 J and 147.9 J respectively.

claim: The R750 specimen exhibits a reduced impact energy of 75.9±22.3 J, elongation of 17.3±3.6%, and a substantially lower crack propagation energy of 51.51 J, resulting in a significantly shortened toughness propagation stage compared to R650.
evidence_text: The impact energy of the R750 specimen decreases to 75.9 ± 22.3 J, but the elongation remains at 17.3 ± 3.6 %. At this point, Ep is greatly reduced to 51.51 J, and the toughness propagation stage is significantly shortened.

claim: High-temperature rolled specimens R850 and R950 display brittle impact behavior with impact energies of only 5.6±0.6 J and 4.6±0.2 J, elongations of 8.3±0.1% and 3.5±0.8%, and impact load-displacement curves that exhibit a steep single peak with nearly vanished crack initiation and propagation energies, confirming unstable crack propagation immediately after initiation.
evidence_text: the impact energies of R850 and R950 are only 5.6 ± 0.6 J and 4.6 ± 0.2 J, respectively, and their elongations drop to 8.3 ± 0.1 % and 3.5 ± 0.8 % (Fig. 5d). Their impact curves (Fig. 5c) show a steep single peak, with Ei and Ep almost disappearing—this confirms that cracks propagate unstably immediately after initiation.

claim: As rolling temperature increases, tensile strength rises steadily from 868.7 MPa for R650 to 1254.5 MPa for R950, while the strength-plasticity product (SPP) drops sharply from 16.51 GPa·% to 4.4 GPa·%, indicating a trade-off between strength and ductility.
evidence_text: Tensile strength rises steadily as rolling temperature increases from 868.7 MPa in R650 to 1254.5 MPa in R950. However, this comes at the cost of significant losses in plasticity and toughness, and the SPP drops sharply from 16.51 GPa⋅% to 4.4 GPa⋅%.

claim: The work hardening rate (θ) curves of low-temperature rolled specimens (R650, R750) exhibit a characteristic hump shape, which originates from the synergistic effect of the carbide/ferrite composite lamellae where carbides with diameters <250 nm pin dislocations via the Orowan mechanism, increasing dislocation density, improving strain hardening capacity, and delaying necking.
evidence_text: The work hardening curve of the low-temperature rolling zone specimen shows a typical hump shape. … This phenomenon originates from the synergistic effect of the carbide/ferrite composite lamellae, in which carbides (diameter <250 nm) pin dislocations via the Orowan mechanism, leading to an increase in dislocation density that improves strain hardening capacity and delays necking.

claim: Transmission electron microscopy and EDS on R650 confirm the presence of carbides M3C, M23C6, and M7C3, which are concentrated outside the δ-ferrite lamellae in the original martensite regions as products of element enrichment after martensite fragmentation, and their massive formation leads to new ferrite in adjacent low-carbon zones, creating carbide-dispersed ferrite composite lamellae.
evidence_text: As shown in the TEM and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) images of each group of R650 in Fig. 7, … the existence of various carbides such as M3C, M23C6, and M7C3 can be confirmed, and these carbides are mainly concentrated outside the δ-ferrite lamellae. … these carbides accumulate in the original martensite regions as products of element enrichment after martensite fragmentation. The massive formation of carbides causes new ferrite to form in the low-carbon regions near them, eventually forming composite lamellae with carbides dispersed in the ferrite matrix.

claim: When the rolling temperature increases from 650°C to 750°C, Ostwald ripening occurs: small carbide particles with high interfacial energy dissolve and diffuse via grain boundaries and dislocations to the surface of larger particles, resulting in a characteristic distribution of coarse carbides at grain boundaries and fine carbides inside grains.
evidence_text: When the rolling temperature rose from 650°C to 750°C, the diffusion coefficients of elements such as C, Mn and Cr increased. Small-sized carbide particles with high interfacial energy dissolve into the ferrite matrix, then diffuse through paths like grain boundaries and dislocations to the surface of large-sized carbide particles with low interfacial energy, where they precipitate to complete the ripening process. The distribution characteristic of ‘coarse carbides at grain boundaries + fine carbides inside grains’ is attributed to … carbides at grain boundaries have higher energy and shorter diffusion paths, which leads to more significant ripening behavior.

claim: In the high-temperature rolling zone (850–950°C), accelerated carbide dissolution occurs within the δ-ferrite + austenite two-phase region, where enhanced austenite stability and increased carbon solubility lead to a microstructure dominated by δ-ferrite lamellae and honeycomb martensite lamellae without observable carbides.
evidence_text: In the high-temperature rolling zone, high temperature further increases the diffusion coefficients of atoms, significantly accelerating carbide dissolution. This temperature range lies within the δ-ferrite (δ) + austenite (γ) two-phase region, where the stability of austenite is notably enhanced. … As a result, no obvious carbides were observed at this rolling temperature, and a microstructure dominated by δ-ferrite lamellae and honeycomb martensite lamellae was finally formed.

claim: In low-temperature rolled steels, dynamic recovery (DRV) is dominant with high dislocation density and increased KAM values; in high-temperature rolled steels, subgrain boundaries transform into high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs) replacing DRV, and deformation-induced ferrite transformation (DIFT) produces submicron equiaxed ferrite grains with higher hardness due to solid solution strengthening by C and Mn and Hall-Petch effect, surrounded by a martensite matrix.
evidence_text: In the low-temperature rolling zone, … the work hardening effect significantly exceeds the dynamic softening effect, leading to a notable increase in the KAM value, which indicates that dynamic recovery (DRV) is the dominant softening mechanism. … In the high-temperature rolling zone, … a part of austenite became unstable due to increased plastic deformation storage energy and decomposed into ferrite synchronously during deformation. After rolling, the residual austenite transformed into martensite, resulting in equiaxed ferrite grains being surrounded by the martensite matrix. Compared with dynamically recrystallized grains, these DIF have smaller sizes (submicron level) and higher hardness. … The solid solution strengthening effect caused by high C content, combined with grain boundary strengthening (Hall-Petch effect), makes its hardness higher than that of recrystallized ferrite.

claim: TEM and SAED/EDS analysis of R650 steel identified multiple carbide types, including M3C, M23C6, and M7C3.
evidence_text: Fig. 7. TEM micrographs and SAED (selected area electron diffraction) patterns, along with EDS results, of the R650 steel: (a) Carbides, (b) M3C, (c) M$_{23}$C$_6$, (d) M$_7$C$_3$.

claim: Spherical carbide particles near a crack tip increase the effective curvature radius of the crack tip, thereby preventing stress concentration because the stress concentration factor is inversely proportional to the curvature radius.
evidence_text: When the crack propagates near the spherical particles, the smooth surface of the particles interacts with the crack, increasing the effective curvature radius of the crack tip and thus preventing stress concentration. According to the theory of elasticity, the stress concentration factor is inversely proportional to the curvature radius—an increase in radius directly reduces the local stress peak at the crack tip.

claim: In R750, decreased carbide proportion and particle coarsening reduce crack deflection resistance, weaken the blunting effect (increased stress intensity factor), diminish Orowan mechanism via lower carbide density and larger spacing, and reduce microvoid nucleation sites, leading to approximately 60% lower impact absorption energy compared to R650.
evidence_text: This change in carbides leads to a reduction in crack deflection resistance, making it easier for cracks to propagate in a straight line and thus lowering energy absorption. It also affects the blunting effect: coarse particles have a weakened ability to disperse stress at the crack tip, and the reduced tip curvature radius causes an increase in the stress intensity factor. Meanwhile, the decrease in carbide quantity results in lower carbide density and larger spacing, reducing the number of sites that hinder dislocation movement and overall weakening the Orowan mechanism. Finally, the number of microvoid nucleation sites in the horizontal direction decreases; fewer ultra-fine carbides lead to less microcrack initiation, weakening the energy dissipation mechanism of secondary cracks. Therefore, the impact absorption energy of R750 decreased by approximately 60 % compared with R650.
